annotations.text: Several factors are likely to have contributed: difficulties with adherence, an interaction between rifampin and ritonavir, and problems with dosing.
Mutations associated with resistance to protease inhibitors and NRTIs, such as lamivudine (M184V) and thymidine analogues, are likely to have developed in this patient.
When it is determined that antiretroviral therapy has failed in a child, a full assessment of the possible reasons for failure should be conducted.
A change in the drug regimen should be guided by the patient's drug history and genotyping (when available).
It is preferable to switch to three or at least two active agents.
Whether the failed regimen was an NNRTI-based regimen or a protease-inhibitor–based regimen will determine which active agents should make up the new regimen.
HIV that is resistant to older protease inhibitors, such as lopinavir boosted with ritonavir, may still be susceptible to newer protease inhibitors, such as darunavir and tipranavir.
In this patient, multiple protease-inhibitor mutations were detected. Since the child had not previously received an NNRTI, a simple switch to two NRTIs and an NNRTI might be appropriate.
Alternatively, a regimen that includes an NNRTI plus a newer protease inhibitor might also be effective.
The mutations seen in this patient's virus confer intermediate resistance to darunavir and tipranavir.
Treatment regimens that fail and that include lamivudine, such as the regimen in this child, select for the M184V mutation, which is one of the first mutations to occur and is present in this case.
The M184V mutation reduces the fitness of viruses and their ability to replicate.
In the absence of K65R and K70E mutations, tenofovir is an ideal agent to continue or to introduce as the second NRTI.
Didanosine may be used in combination with lamivudine or emtricitabine when thymidine-analogue mutations are present and abacavir cannot be used.

Ideally, after counseling this patient and his family, and if it is affordable, special permission should be sought — through local pharmaceutical regulatory authorities or direct application on compassionate grounds to the drug company — to use ritonavir-boosted darunavir.
Although multiple NRTI mutations and intermediate resistance are present, didanosine may be used. Also, lamivudine may be retained.
In situations in which resources are limited, however, ritonavir-boosted darunavir might not be readily available or affordable.

If resistance to multiple protease inhibitors occurs, options might be to switch to a less durable regimen, such as efavirenz, didanosine, and lamivudine, or to try a holding regimen such as lamivudine monotherapy, which has shown some promising results in a small study involving adults.
Viral resistance in children may be prevented by early identification of HIV-infected children and the initiation of treatment.
Clinicians must be familiar with drug interactions and prescribe accordingly. Doses must be adjusted to account for growth in children.

Regular monitoring of treatment responses should be in place. Laboratory investigations, such as viral-load monitoring and resistance testing, must become more affordable to programs to guide treatment decisions.
